result: The air in my apartment smells faintly of cardamom and burnt toast, evidence of this morning’s impulsive decision to bake something—anything—before coffee had even finished dripping. I never measure spices anymore; I let the scent tell me when to stop. The same trust extends to books: I open them at random, hunting for a sentence that feels like a loose thread I can tug until the whole day unravels into possibility. Yesterday it was Clarice Lispector: “I am not an intellectual, I write with my body.” I underlined it three times, then went running along the East River, pretending each footfall was punctuation in a story my bones were drafting. The skyline across the water looked like a sentence interrupted by impatient clouds. I waved at a stranger’s dog and accidentally invented a backstory in which the dog was once a librarian in a past life; the owner smiled as if he’d overheard. By dusk I had crumbs in my pocket, a blister on my heel, and the certainty that time is not a clock but an oven: it only works if you keep putting things inside to warm—memories, questions, the way your mother’s voice lingers on the answering machine you refuse to replace. I came home, added cardamom to the dough again, and let it rise while I typed nonsense into my phone, trusting the yeast and the words to triple their volume overnight. This morning the loaf cracked open like a book spine, steam curling out in scented Arabic I can’t read but somehow understand.

Yesterday it was Clarice Lispector: “I am not an intellectual, I write with my body.” I underlined it three times, then went running along the East River, pretending each footfall was punctuation in a story my bones were drafting. The skyline across the water looked like a sentence interrupted by impatient clouds. I waved at a stranger’s dog and accidentally invented a backstory in which the dog was once a librarian in a past life; the owner smiled as if he’d overheard. I came home, added cardamom to the dough again, and let it rise while I typed nonsense into my phone, trusting the yeast and the words to triple their volume overnight. This morning the loaf cracked open like a book spine, steam curling out in scented Arabic I can’t read but somehow understand.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
